verses.text: And it came to pass, when the king sat in his house, and the LORD had given him rest round about from all his enemies; That the king said unto Nathan the prophet, See now, I dwell in an house of cedar, but the ark of God dwelleth within curtains. And Nathan said to the king, Go, do all that is in thine heart; for the LORD is with thee. Guidance: The scene: David is settled in his cedar palace (the one Hiram of Tyre had built him in chapter 5), the wars are over, the Philistines are subdued, the ark is in Jerusalem. David's restlessness in peace is what produces the chapter. He looks at his own house — paneled with imported cedar, royal grade — and at the ark's house — a tent pitched in the city of David. The contrast troubles him. The king's place is more magnificent than the LORD's. Without making it a formal proposal, he raises the issue with Nathan the prophet: 'I dwell in an house of cedar, but the ark of God dwelleth within curtains.' Nathan's first response is the friendly approval a court prophet often gives: 'Go, do all that is in thine heart; for the LORD is with thee.' Nathan speaks pastorally without consulting God; he assumes the king's pious instinct must be God's will. He will be corrected by the LORD overnight. The chapter is teaching us that even a faithful prophet can speak too quickly when he speaks from his own confidence in the king's heart rather than from the LORD's actual word. Sometimes the right response is 'let me ask' rather than 'go ahead.'

And it came to pass that night, that the word of the LORD came unto Nathan, saying, Go and tell my servant David, Thus saith the LORD, Shalt thou build me an house for me to dwell in? Whereas I have not dwelt in any house since the time that I brought up the children of Israel out of Egypt, even to this day, but have walked in a tent and in a tabernacle. In all the places wherein I have walked with all the children of Israel spake I a word with any of the tribes of Israel, whom I commanded to feed my people Israel, saying, Why build ye not me an house of cedar? Guidance: 'That night.' Within hours of Nathan's pastoral 'go ahead,' the LORD corrects him. The word comes by direct revelation. The LORD's argument is surprising. He does not say 'No, you may not build me a house.' He asks a question and then makes a historical observation. 'Shalt thou build me an house for me to dwell in?' — implied: did I ask? Then the history: 'I have not dwelt in any house since the time that I brought up the children of Israel out of Egypt, even to this day, but have walked in a tent and in a tabernacle.' The LORD identifies Himself as a mobile God, present with his people in a portable tent for four hundred years, deliberately not having a permanent address. 'In all the places wherein I have walked with all the children of Israel spake I a word with any of the tribes of Israel… saying, Why build ye not me an house of cedar?' The LORD has never once complained about His housing. He has never solicited an upgrade. The LORD's question to Nathan is whether David's instinct, however reverent, is grounded in something the LORD has actually requested. The lesson is deep. The LORD's people often want to give Him gifts the giver values more than the LORD values. The proper question before any major religious building project is not 'is it pious?' but 'has the LORD asked for it?' The chapter is teaching us that worship works best when our service to God is in the categories God Himself has invited, not in the categories we think He must want.

Now therefore so shalt thou say unto my servant David, Thus saith the LORD of hosts, I took thee from the sheepcote, from following the sheep, to be ruler over my people, over Israel: And I was with thee whithersoever thou wentest, and have cut off all thine enemies out of thy sight, and have made thee a great name, like unto the name of the great men that are in the earth. Moreover I will appoint a place for my people Israel, and will plant them, that they may dwell in a place of their own, and move no more; neither shall the children of wickedness afflict them any more, as beforetime, And as since the time that I commanded judges to be over my people Israel, and have caused thee to rest from all thine enemies. Also the LORD telleth thee that he will make thee an house. Guidance: The LORD reframes the entire transaction. David proposed to build a house for the LORD. The LORD proposes to build a house for David. The reversal is breathtaking. (1) Past: 'I took thee from the sheepcote, from following the sheep, to be ruler over my people.' The LORD reminds David where he started — a shepherd boy at Bethlehem. (2) 'I was with thee whithersoever thou wentest, and have cut off all thine enemies, and have made thee a great name.' Three past accomplishments — presence, victory, reputation — all the LORD's gifts, not David's earnings. (3) Future: 'I will appoint a place for my people Israel, and will plant them, that they may dwell in a place of their own, and move no more.' The LORD will settle His people permanently. (4) 'I will cause thee to rest from all thine enemies.' Continued rest is promised. (5) Then the turn: 'Also the LORD telleth thee that he will make thee an house.' The Hebrew bayit ('house') begins to pun in this verse. David wanted to build a bayit (temple); the LORD will build a bayit (dynasty). The same word, two meanings. The LORD is not giving David a building; He is giving him a family-line. The play on the word will run through the whole chapter. The lesson is one of the great ones of biblical theology: God always out-gives the offerer. David proposed a building project of stones and cedar; God answers with a dynasty of generations and a Son of David who will reign forever. Whenever we set out to give God something, He has a habit of arranging the conversation so that the gift comes the other direction.

And when thy days be fulfilled, and thou shalt sleep with thy fathers, I will set up thy seed after thee, which shall proceed out of thy bowels, and I will establish his kingdom. He shall build an house for my name, and I will stablish the throne of his kingdom for ever. Guidance: The covenant proper begins. The structure is dynastic. (1) 'When thy days be fulfilled, and thou shalt sleep with thy fathers' — David will die a natural death (unlike Saul on Gilboa); the LORD promises a peaceful end and burial with the patriarchs. (2) 'I will set up thy seed after thee, which shall proceed out of thy bowels, and I will establish his kingdom.' A literal son of David, biologically descended, will inherit. (3) 'He shall build an house for my name.' Solomon, the immediate referent, will build the temple. The LORD is not refusing the temple; He is reassigning it from the father to the son. David has the desire; Solomon will have the execution. (4) 'I will stablish the throne of his kingdom for ever.' Le-olam — 'unto the age,' permanent. The first 'for ever' lands here, and it will recur four more times in the chapter. The covenant goes beyond Solomon; the throne itself is permanent. The Christian reading is that this verse points beyond any immediate Davidic king to Christ, the ultimate Son of David, whose throne is literally eternal. The Old Testament reading is that the Davidic dynasty has a divine guarantee no other Israelite king will have. Even when the dynasty falls into exile in 586 BC, the promise will hold — the line will survive, and at the right time the LORD will fulfill it through Jesus of Nazareth. The chapter is establishing the theological foundation for everything subsequent prophets will say about the messianic king to come.

I will be his father, and he shall be my son. If he commit iniquity, I will chasten him with the rod of men, and with the stripes of the children of men: But my mercy shall not depart away from him, as I took it from Saul, whom I put away before thee. Guidance: 'I will be his father, and he shall be my son.' One of the most consequential sentences in the Hebrew Bible. The LORD adopts the Davidic king as His own son. The relationship is adoptive, not biological, but the language is paternal. Psalm 2:7 will quote and develop this — 'Thou art my son; this day have I begotten thee.' The New Testament will apply the verse directly to Jesus (Heb 1:5 cites it of Christ's sonship). The Davidic king is, in some sense, both the LORD's chosen ruler and the LORD's chosen son, and the relationship between them is family. Then the realism: 'If he commit iniquity, I will chasten him with the rod of men, and with the stripes of the children of men.' The LORD anticipates that Davidic kings will sin (and they will — many of them disastrously). The LORD will discipline them through human means — historical consequences, defeated armies, foreign invasions. The covenant is not unconditional in personal behavior; bad kings will get punished. But the next sentence is the heart of the covenant: 'My mercy shall not depart away from him, as I took it from Saul.' The hesed (covenant love) of the LORD will not be revoked from the Davidic line in the way it was revoked from Saul. Individual Davidic kings may fail and be punished; the line itself is secure. This is the most important promise in the chapter for the theology of biblical hope. Israel will sin; the dynasty will be exiled; Jerusalem will fall — but the LORD's covenant with David is not contingent on Davidic perfection. Hesed remains. The Son of David who will come, Jesus, fulfills the promise without ever needing the chastening clause; He is the one Davidic king whose obedience completes the covenant from the inside.

And thine house and thy kingdom shall be established for ever before thee: thy throne shall be established for ever. Guidance: The covenant climax. 'Thine house' (the dynasty), 'thy kingdom' (the realm), 'thy throne' (the rule) — three terms for the Davidic monarchy — all 'established for ever.' The Hebrew 'forever' (le-olam) appears three times in this verse alone. The LORD is hammering home that the promise is not provisional. The chapter has now constructed the theological infrastructure for messianic hope. Every later prophet — Isaiah, Jeremiah, Ezekiel, Micah, Zechariah — will preach against the background of this verse. When the prophet says 'a king will reign on David's throne forever,' he is invoking 2 Samuel 7:16. When the angel Gabriel tells Mary that her son will receive 'the throne of his father David… and he shall reign over the house of Jacob for ever' (Luke 1:32-33), he is quoting this verse. The verse is the source code of Christian messianic theology. The chapter cannot be over-quoted in the rest of scripture. The whole Old Testament's expectation of a coming king, the whole New Testament's claim that Jesus is that king, traces back to this sentence. The promise is unilateral, unconditional in dynasty, eternal in scope. The Davidic king is, by this verse, the LORD's own son, the LORD's own permanent appointment, the focal point of the LORD's plan for Israel and the world.

According to all these words, and according to all this vision, so did Nathan speak unto David. Guidance: One verse, the report. Nathan delivers the LORD's word to David. The narrator does not record the conversation; he assumes the previous verses are the message. The verb 'speak' here is the workmanlike verb of a prophet who has been corrected and now returns to the king with the actual word. Nathan does not modify, soften, or interpret. He delivers verbatim. The chapter is showing us the difference between Nathan's first response in v3 ('go ahead, the LORD is with thee') and his second in v17 (the LORD's actual word). A good prophet is willing to be corrected and to deliver the correction to the king he had previously affirmed. Nathan will appear again in the David narrative — chapter 12, when he delivers the LORD's word about David's sin with Bathsheba; and 1 Kings 1, when he intervenes for Solomon's succession. His ministry will be defined by exactly this willingness to bring the LORD's word to David even when it is unwelcome. The chapter is establishing the model of prophetic ministry at court: not a court flatterer, not a chaplain who blesses everything, but a faithful messenger who corrects himself when necessary and delivers what the LORD actually said.

Then went king David in, and sat before the LORD, and he said, Who am I, O Lord GOD? and what is my house, that thou hast brought me hitherto? And this was yet a small thing in thy sight, O Lord GOD; but thou hast spoken also of thy servant's house for a great while to come. And is this the manner of man, O Lord GOD? Guidance: David's response is one of the most beautiful prayers in the Old Testament. He goes to the ark-tent ('went in') and sits — the Hebrew yashav is unusual; one stood or prostrated to pray, but David sits, as if too overwhelmed to remain on his feet. He addresses God as Adonai YHWH ('Lord GOD' — the rare double divine name that signals deep reverence). The opening is one of the great prayer-openings of scripture: 'Who am I, O Lord GOD? and what is my house, that thou hast brought me hitherto?' The same 'who am I?' he used when Saul offered him a daughter (1 Sam 18:18). The wilderness humility is intact even on the throne. (Hebrew mi anochi.) The contrast between his perception of himself ('Who am I?') and what the LORD has just promised (a forever-dynasty) is the engine of the prayer. Then his amazement at the scale: 'And this was yet a small thing in thy sight, O Lord GOD; but thou hast spoken also of thy servant's house for a great while to come.' Everything the LORD has done so far (anointing, victories, throne) is now classified by David as 'a small thing' compared to the dynastic promise just made. 'Is this the manner of man, O Lord GOD?' The Hebrew is ambiguous: 'Is this the law for man?' or 'Is this how a man is normally treated?' Either way, David is saying that the LORD's covenant with him does not fit normal human-relational categories. There is no analog. He has not received a king's bonus; he has received a divine adoption-of-a-dynasty no human relationship ever produces. The prayer is suffused with wonder.

Then went king David in, and sat before the LORD, and he said, Who am I, O Lord GOD? and what is my house, that thou hast brought me hitherto? And this was yet a small thing in thy sight, O Lord GOD; but thou hast spoken also of thy servant's house for a great while to come. And is this the manner of man, O Lord GOD? Guidance: David's response is one of the most beautiful prayers in the Old Testament. He goes to the ark-tent ('went in') and sits — the Hebrew yashav is unusual; one stood or prostrated to pray, but David sits, as if too overwhelmed to remain on his feet. He addresses God as Adonai YHWH ('Lord GOD' — the rare double divine name that signals deep reverence). The opening is one of the great prayer-openings of scripture: 'Who am I, O Lord GOD? and what is my house, that thou hast brought me hitherto?' The same 'who am I?' he used when Saul offered him a daughter (1 Sam 18:18). The wilderness humility is intact even on the throne. (Hebrew mi anochi.) The contrast between his perception of himself ('Who am I?') and what the LORD has just promised (a forever-dynasty) is the engine of the prayer. Then his amazement at the scale: 'And this was yet a small thing in thy sight, O Lord GOD; but thou hast spoken also of thy servant's house for a great while to come.' Everything the LORD has done so far (anointing, victories, throne) is now classified by David as 'a small thing' compared to the dynastic promise just made. 'Is this the manner of man, O Lord GOD?' The Hebrew is ambiguous: 'Is this the law for man?' or 'Is this how a man is normally treated?' Either way, David is saying that the LORD's covenant with him does not fit normal human-relational categories. There is no analog. He has not received a king's bonus; he has received a divine adoption-of-a-dynasty no human relationship ever produces. The prayer is suffused with wonder.

And what can David say more unto thee? for thou, Lord GOD, knowest thy servant. For thy word's sake, and according to thine own heart, hast thou done all these great things, to make thy servant know them. Guidance: 'What can David say more unto thee?' The prayer reaches the limit of speech. Words have failed to match the gift. The pivot: 'thou, Lord GOD, knowest thy servant.' David doesn't need to articulate what God already knows. The prayer becomes a confession of God's intimate knowledge. (Compare Psalm 139's 'O LORD, thou hast searched me, and known me.' The same David, the same wonder at being fully known.) Then the diagnosis of why the LORD has done all this: 'For thy word's sake, and according to thine own heart.' The LORD has not acted because David earned it; the LORD has acted because of His own word (the prior promises made to Israel and to David) and His own heart (His sovereign love-character). The chapter is teaching us how to receive a covenant: name the giver's motive (the LORD's word, the LORD's heart), not the receiver's worthiness. 'To make thy servant know them.' The reception is itself the LORD's gift. David is grateful even for the privilege of being told what the LORD intends to do.

Wherefore thou art great, O LORD God: for there is none like thee, neither is there any God beside thee, according to all that we have heard with our ears. And what one nation in the earth is like thy people, even like Israel, whom God went to redeem for a people to himself, and to make him a name, and to do for you great things and terrible, for thy land, before thy people, which thou redeemedst to thee from Egypt, from the nations and their gods? For thou hast confirmed to thyself thy people Israel to be a people unto thee for ever: and thou, LORD, art become their God. Guidance: David shifts from personal gratitude to corporate doxology. (1) 'Thou art great, O LORD God: for there is none like thee, neither is there any God beside thee.' Two declarations: God's greatness, and God's uniqueness. The two great themes of Hebrew monotheism. 'According to all that we have heard with our ears' — the doctrine is not new; it is the inherited testimony of Israel, now confirmed once more by what the LORD has done for David. (2) Then the corporate amazement: 'What one nation in the earth is like thy people, even like Israel?' Israel is unique because of what God did for Israel — redeemed them, made them His own, did 'great things and terrible' (i.e., awesome) for them. The Exodus is named: 'redeemedst to thee from Egypt, from the nations and their gods.' The Davidic covenant is being read against the Exodus background; the LORD who freed Israel is the same LORD who now establishes the Davidic dynasty. The two are continuous; the Davidic king is the next stage of the Exodus people. (3) 'Thou hast confirmed to thyself thy people Israel to be a people unto thee for ever: and thou, LORD, art become their God.' The corporate covenant ('I will be their God, and they shall be my people') is reaffirmed within David's personal covenant. The chapter is showing us a king who, given a personal promise, immediately broadens the prayer to include the entire people. The Davidic covenant is not in tension with the Sinai covenant; it is the Sinai covenant's royal continuation. Faithful gratitude for personal blessing always widens to include the people of God around the believer.

And now, O LORD God, the word that thou hast spoken concerning thy servant, and concerning his house, establish it for ever, and do as thou hast said. And let thy name be magnified for ever, saying, The LORD of hosts is the God over Israel: and let the house of thy servant David be established before thee. For thou, O LORD of hosts, God of Israel, hast revealed to thy servant, saying, I will build thee an house: therefore hath thy servant found in his heart to pray this prayer unto thee. And now, O Lord GOD, thou art that God, and thy words be true, and thou hast promised this goodness unto thy servant: Therefore now let it please thee to bless the house of thy servant, that it may continue for ever before thee: for thou, Lord GOD, hast spoken it: and with thy blessing let the house of thy servant be blessed for ever. Guidance: The prayer closes with a series of requests, all of which are essentially 'do what you have said.' (1) 'The word that thou hast spoken concerning thy servant, and concerning his house, establish it for ever, and do as thou hast said.' Notice the prayer is not for extra; it is for the LORD to fulfill what He has already promised. The most faithful prayer is to ask God for what He has already declared He will do. (2) 'Let thy name be magnified for ever, saying, The LORD of hosts is the God over Israel.' The motive of the request is the LORD's name, not David's name. (3) 'For thou, O LORD of hosts… hast revealed to thy servant, saying, I will build thee an house: therefore hath thy servant found in his heart to pray this prayer unto thee.' David explicitly names the connection: the LORD's revealed promise produces the prayer. Faithful prayer is response to revealed word; it is not free-form wish. (4) 'Thou art that God, and thy words be true.' Affirmation of God's reliability. (5) The closing benediction: 'Let it please thee to bless the house of thy servant, that it may continue for ever before thee… and with thy blessing let the house of thy servant be blessed for ever.' Two more 'forevers' bring the chapter to nine total. The prayer rests on the LORD's promise and asks the LORD to do what the LORD already said. The chapter ends with the king-in-prayer responding to the LORD-in-promise, the relational shape of the covenant on full display. The throne of David has now been established not just politically (chapters 1-6) but theologically (chapter 7). The rest of 2 Samuel will show us this throne tested by sin (chapters 11-12) and by rebellion (chapters 13-20). But the covenant of chapter 7 will hold. The Son of David promised in v12-16 will, eventually, sit on the throne for ever. The chapter is the hinge of all subsequent biblical theology. Hold every promise of the messianic line against the background of these words; every later prophet draws his hope from this chapter; the New Testament's whole picture of Christ as the eternal king of David's house is the working-out of the verses you have just read.
